sections: In computer simulations of board games
Generative artificial intelligence in video games
first_paragraph: Artificial intelligence (AI) in [video games](/wiki/Video_games "Video games") refers to the computational systems that control [non-player characters](/wiki/Non-player_characters "Non-player characters") (NPCs), generate dynamic game behavior, or simulate strategic decision-making. In practice, the term covers a broad range of techniques drawn from [computer science](/wiki/Computer_science "Computer science"), [control theory](/wiki/Control_theory "Control theory"), and [robotics](/wiki/Robotics "Robotics") rather than “true” [artificial intelligence](/wiki/Artificial_intelligence "Artificial intelligence"). Moreover, academic AI—aimed at developing autonomous reasoning or learning—is distinct from game AI, whose goal is to deliver engaging, believable, and efficient behavior for players. The term *CPU* in video games refer to the AI that control a NPC that could be a [playable character](/wiki/Playable_character "Playable character") in some other situations.

Artificial intelligence (AI) in [video games](/wiki/Video_games "Video games") refers to the computational systems that control [non-player characters](/wiki/Non-player_characters "Non-player characters") (NPCs), generate dynamic game behavior, or simulate strategic decision-making. In practice, the term covers a broad range of techniques drawn from [computer science](/wiki/Computer_science "Computer science"), [control theory](/wiki/Control_theory "Control theory"), and [robotics](/wiki/Robotics "Robotics") rather than “true” [artificial intelligence](/wiki/Artificial_intelligence "Artificial intelligence"). Moreover, academic AI—aimed at developing autonomous reasoning or learning—is distinct from game AI, whose goal is to deliver engaging, believable, and efficient behavior for players. The term *CPU* in video games refer to the AI that control a NPC that could be a [playable character](/wiki/Playable_character "Playable character") in some other situations.

In general, game AI does not, as might be thought and sometimes is depicted to be the case, mean a realization of an artificial person corresponding to an NPC in the manner of the [Turing test](/wiki/Turing_test "Turing test") or an [artificial general intelligence](/wiki/Artificial_general_intelligence "Artificial general intelligence").

## Overview

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Artificial_intelligence_in_video_games&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Overview")]

The term *game AI* is used to refer to a broad set of [algorithms](/wiki/Algorithm "Algorithm") that also include techniques from [control theory](/wiki/Control_theory "Control theory"), [robotics](/wiki/Robotics "Robotics"), [computer graphics](/wiki/Computer_graphics "Computer graphics") and [computer science](/wiki/Computer_science "Computer science") in general, and so video game AI may often not constitute "true AI" in that such techniques do not necessarily facilitate computer learning or other standard criteria, only constituting "automated computation" or a predetermined and limited set of responses to a predetermined and limited set of inputs.[[1]](#cite_note-AtlanticAIMeaningless-1)[[2]](#cite_note-MITPRProblem-2)[[3]](#cite_note-AIMattersWhoSpeaks-3)

Some commentators in the video game industry have claimed that advances in game AI have transformed how players interact with digital systems more broadly. However, such claims remain disputed. Marketing discussions sometimes attribute human-like traits to game AI—such as personality or creative autonomy—but these are often scripted behaviors designed to mimic complexity rather than emergent cognition.[[1]](#cite_note-AtlanticAIMeaningless-1)[[2]](#cite_note-MITPRProblem-2)[[3]](#cite_note-AIMattersWhoSpeaks-3)[[4]](#cite_note-4)[[5]](#cite_note-5)[[6]](#cite_note-6)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Artificial_intelligence_in_video_games&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: History")]

Game playing was an area of research in AI from its inception. One of the first examples of AI is the computerized game of [Nim](/wiki/Nim "Nim") made in 1941 and published in 1942. Despite being advanced technology in the year it was made, 20 years before *[Pong](/wiki/Pong "Pong")*, the game took the form of a relatively small box and was able to regularly win games even against highly skilled players of the game.[[7]](#cite_note-Redheffer-1948-7) In 1951, using the [Ferranti Mark 1](/wiki/Ferranti_Mark_1 "Ferranti Mark 1") machine of the [University of Manchester](/wiki/University_of_Manchester "University of Manchester"), [Christopher Strachey](/wiki/Christopher_Strachey "Christopher Strachey") wrote a [checkers](/wiki/Draughts "Draughts") program and [Dietrich Prinz](/wiki/Dietrich_Prinz "Dietrich Prinz") wrote one for [chess](/wiki/Chess "Chess").[[8]](#cite_note-8) These were among the first computer programs ever written. [Arthur Samuel](/wiki/Arthur_Samuel_(computer_scientist) "Arthur Samuel (computer scientist)")'s checkers program, developed in the middle 1950s and early 1960s, eventually achieved sufficient skill to challenge a respectable amateur.[[9]](#cite_note-9) Work on checkers and chess would culminate in the defeat of [Garry Kasparov](/wiki/Garry_Kasparov "Garry Kasparov") by [IBM](/wiki/IBM "IBM")'s [Deep Blue](/wiki/Deep_Blue_(chess_computer) "Deep Blue (chess computer)") computer in 1997.[[10]](#cite_note-10) The first [video games](/wiki/Video_game "Video game") developed in the 1960s and early 1970s, like *[Spacewar!](/wiki/Spacewar! "Spacewar!")*, *[Pong](/wiki/Pong "Pong")*, and *[Gotcha](/wiki/Gotcha_(arcade_game) "Gotcha (arcade game)")* (1973), were games implemented on [discrete logic](/wiki/Discrete_logic "Discrete logic") and strictly based on the competition of two players, without AI.

Games that featured a [single player](/wiki/Single_player "Single player") mode with enemies started appearing in the 1970s. The first notable ones for the [arcade](/wiki/Arcade_game "Arcade game") appeared in 1974: the [Taito](/wiki/Taito_Corporation "Taito Corporation") game *[Speed Race](/wiki/Speed_Race "Speed Race")* ([racing video game](/wiki/Racing_video_game "Racing video game")) and the [Atari](/wiki/Atari "Atari") games *[Qwak](/wiki/Qwak!_(arcade_game) "Qwak! (arcade game)")* (duck hunting [light gun shooter](/wiki/Light_gun_shooter "Light gun shooter")) and *[Pursuit](/wiki/Pursuit_(arcade_game) "Pursuit (arcade game)")* ([fighter aircraft dogfighting simulator](/wiki/Combat_flight_simulation_game "Combat flight simulation game")). Two text-based computer games, *[Star Trek](/wiki/Star_Trek_(1971_video_game) "Star Trek (1971 video game)")* (1971) and *[Hunt the Wumpus](/wiki/Hunt_the_Wumpus "Hunt the Wumpus")* (1973), also had enemies. Enemy movement was based on stored patterns. The incorporation of [microprocessors](/wiki/Microprocessor "Microprocessor") would allow more computation and random elements overlaid into movement patterns.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f2/GLtronwin.jpg/250px-GLtronwin.jpg)](/wiki/File:GLtronwin.jpg)

Light cycle characters compete to be the last one riding in *[GLtron](/wiki/GLtron "GLtron").*

It was during the [golden age of video arcade games](/wiki/Golden_age_of_video_arcade_games "Golden age of video arcade games") that the idea of AI opponents was largely popularized, due to the success of *[Space Invaders](/wiki/Space_Invaders "Space Invaders")* (1978), which sported an increasing difficulty level, distinct movement patterns, and in-game events dependent on [hash functions](/wiki/Hash_function "Hash function") based on the player's input. *[Galaxian](/wiki/Galaxian "Galaxian")* (1979) added more complex and varied enemy movements, including maneuvers by individual enemies who break out of formation. *[Pac-Man](/wiki/Pac-Man "Pac-Man")* (1980) introduced AI patterns to [maze games](/wiki/List_of_maze_video_games "List of maze video games"), with the added quirk of different personalities for each enemy. *[Karate Champ](/wiki/Karate_Champ "Karate Champ")* (1984) later introduced AI patterns to [fighting games](/wiki/Fighting_game "Fighting game"). *[First Queen](/w/index.php?title=First_Queen&action=edit&redlink=1 "First Queen (page does not exist)")* (1988) was a [tactical](/wiki/Tactical_role-playing_game "Tactical role-playing game") [action RPG](/wiki/Action_role-playing_game "Action role-playing game") which featured characters that can be controlled by the computer's AI in following the leader.[[11]](#cite_note-MobyGames-11)[[12]](#cite_note-kuresoft-12) The [role-playing video game](/wiki/Role-playing_video_game "Role-playing video game") *[Dragon Quest IV](/wiki/Dragon_Quest_IV "Dragon Quest IV")* (1990) introduced a "Tactics" system, where the user can adjust the AI routines of [non-player characters](/wiki/Non-player_character "Non-player character") during battle, a concept later introduced to the [action role-playing game](/wiki/Action_role-playing_game "Action role-playing game") genre by *[Secret of Mana](/wiki/Secret_of_Mana "Secret of Mana")* (1993).

Games like *[Madden Football](/wiki/Madden_NFL "Madden NFL")*, *[Earl Weaver Baseball](/wiki/Earl_Weaver_Baseball "Earl Weaver Baseball")* and *[Tony La Russa Baseball](/wiki/Tony_La_Russa_Baseball "Tony La Russa Baseball")* all based their AI in an attempt to duplicate on the computer the coaching or managerial style of the selected celebrity. Madden, Weaver and La Russa all did extensive work with these game development teams to maximize the accuracy of the games.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Later sports titles allowed users to "tune" variables in the AI to produce a player-defined managerial or coaching strategy.

The emergence of new game genres in the 1990s prompted the use of formal AI tools like [finite-state machines](/wiki/Finite-state_machine "Finite-state machine"). [Real-time strategy](/wiki/Real-time_strategy "Real-time strategy") games taxed the AI with many objects, incomplete information, pathfinding problems, real-time decisions and economic planning, among other things.[[13]](#cite_note-13) The first games of the genre had notorious problems. *[Herzog Zwei](/wiki/Herzog_Zwei "Herzog Zwei")* (1989), for example, had almost broken pathfinding and very basic three-state state machines for unit control, and *[Dune II](/wiki/Dune_II "Dune II")* (1992) attacked the players' base in a beeline and used numerous cheats.[[14]](#cite_note-14) Later games in the genre exhibited more sophisticated AI.

Later games have used [bottom-up](/wiki/Top-down_and_bottom-up_design "Top-down and bottom-up design") AI methods, such as the [emergent behaviour](/wiki/Emergent_behaviour "Emergent behaviour") and evaluation of player actions in games like *[Creatures](/wiki/Creatures_(artificial_life_program) "Creatures (artificial life program)")* or *[Black & White](/wiki/Black_%26_White_(video_game) "Black & White (video game)")*. *[Façade](/wiki/Fa%C3%A7ade_(interactive_story) "Façade (interactive story)")* was released in 2005 and used interactive multiple way dialogs and AI as the main aspect of game.

[![A robot goes for the ball and competes in Robocup.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4c/13-06-28-robocup-eindhoven-024.jpg/250px-13-06-28-robocup-eindhoven-024.jpg)](/wiki/File:13-06-28-robocup-eindhoven-024.jpg)

A robot goes for the ball and competes in Robocup

Games have provided an environment for developing artificial intelligence with potential applications beyond gameplay. Examples include [Watson](/wiki/Watson_(artificial_intelligence_software) "Watson (artificial intelligence software)"), a [Jeopardy!](/wiki/Jeopardy! "Jeopardy!")-playing computer; and the [RoboCup](/wiki/RoboCup "RoboCup") tournament, where robots are trained to compete in soccer.[[15]](#cite_note-15)

## Views

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Artificial_intelligence_in_video_games&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Views")]

Many experts[*[who?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Words_to_watch#Unsupported_attributions "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Words to watch")*] complain that the "AI" in the term *game AI* overstates its worth, as game AI is not about [intelligence](/wiki/Intelligence_(trait) "Intelligence (trait)"), and shares few of the objectives of the academic field of AI. Whereas "real AI" addresses fields of machine learning, decision making based on arbitrary data input, and even the ultimate goal of [strong AI](/wiki/Artificial_general_intelligence "Artificial general intelligence") that can reason, "game AI" often consists of a half-dozen rules of thumb, or [heuristics](/wiki/Heuristic_(computer_science) "Heuristic (computer science)"), that are just enough to give a good gameplay experience.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] These rules are designed to create the illusion of intelligence and work off of three core principles: the player desiring to believe that there is human-level intelligence in the games they play, the human urge to anthropomorphize nonhuman entities, and finally the power of expectation enhancing the player experience. These principles are further enhanced in the quality and performance of the game AI, “selling the illusion” through animations that are fluid and not robotic, dialog that fits the situation, giving the AI a reason to exist and a personality beyond the player, and having the appropriate reaction to the situation and environment.[[16]](#cite_note-16) Historically, academic game-AI projects have been relatively separate from commercial products because the academic approaches tended to be simple and non-scalable. Commercial game AI has developed its own set of tools, which have been sufficient to give good performance in many cases.[[17]](#cite_note-Yannakakis2012-17)

Game developers' increasing awareness of academic AI and a growing interest in computer games by the academic community is causing the definition of what counts as AI in a game to become less [idiosyncratic](/wiki/Idiosyncrasy "Idiosyncrasy"). Nevertheless, significant differences between different application domains of AI mean that game AI can still be viewed as a distinct subfield of AI. In particular, the ability to legitimately solve some AI problems in games by [cheating](/wiki/Cheating "Cheating") creates an important distinction. For example, inferring the position of an unseen object from past observations can be a difficult problem when AI is applied to robotics, but in a computer game a NPC can simply look up the position in the game's [scene graph](/wiki/Scene_graph "Scene graph"). Such cheating can lead to unrealistic behavior and so is not always desirable. But its possibility serves to distinguish game AI and leads to new problems to solve, such as when and how to cheat.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The major limitation to strong AI is the inherent depth of thinking and the extreme complexity of the decision-making process. This means that although it would be then theoretically possible to make "smart" AI the problem would take considerable processing power.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### In modern video games

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Artificial_intelligence_in_video_games&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: In modern video games")]

Further information: [Video game bot](/wiki/Video_game_bot "Video game bot")

Game AI/heuristic algorithms are used in a wide variety of quite disparate fields inside a game. The most obvious is in the control of any NPCs in the game, although "scripting" ([decision tree](/wiki/Decision_tree "Decision tree")) is currently the most common means of control.[[18]](#cite_note-18) These handwritten decision trees often result in "artificial stupidity" such as repetitive behavior, loss of immersion, or abnormal behavior in situations the developers did not plan for.[[19]](#cite_note-19)

[Pathfinding](/wiki/Pathfinding "Pathfinding"), another common use for AI, is widely seen in [real-time strategy](/wiki/Real-time_strategy "Real-time strategy") games. Pathfinding is the method for determining how to get a NPC from one point on a map to another, taking into consideration the terrain, obstacles and possibly "[fog of war](/wiki/Fog_of_war#In_video_games "Fog of war")".[[20]](#cite_note-game_ai_revisited-20)[[21]](#cite_note-21) Commercial videogames often use fast and simple "grid-based pathfinding", wherein the terrain is mapped onto a rigid grid of uniform squares and a pathfinding algorithm such as [A\*](/wiki/A*_search_algorithm "A* search algorithm") or [IDA\*](/wiki/IDA* "IDA*") is applied to the grid.[[22]](#cite_note-comprehensive-22)[[23]](#cite_note-23)[[24]](#cite_note-24) Instead of just a rigid grid, some games use irregular polygons and assemble a [navigation mesh](/wiki/Navigation_mesh "Navigation mesh") out of the areas of the map that NPCs can walk to.[[22]](#cite_note-comprehensive-22)[[25]](#cite_note-25) As a third method, it is sometimes convenient for developers to manually select "waypoints" that NPCs should use to navigate; the cost is that such waypoints can create unnatural-looking movement. In addition, waypoints tend to perform worse than navigation meshes in complex environments.[[26]](#cite_note-26)[[27]](#cite_note-cui-27) Beyond static pathfinding, [navigation](/w/index.php?title=Navigation_(AI)&action=edit&redlink=1 "Navigation (AI) (page does not exist)") is a sub-field of Game AI focusing on giving NPCs the capability to navigate in a dynamic environment, finding a path to a target while avoiding collisions with other entities (other NPC, players...) or collaborating with them (group navigation).[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Navigation in dynamic strategy games with large numbers of units, such as [Age of Empires](/wiki/Age_of_Empires_(video_game) "Age of Empires (video game)") (1997) or [Civilization V](/wiki/Civilization_V "Civilization V") (2010), often performs poorly; units often get in the way of other units.[[27]](#cite_note-cui-27)

Rather than improve the Game AI to properly solve a difficult problem in the virtual environment, it is often more cost-effective to just modify the scenario to be more tractable. If pathfinding gets bogged down over a specific obstacle, a developer may just end up moving or deleting the obstacle.[[28]](#cite_note-28) In [Half-Life](/wiki/Half-Life_(video_game) "Half-Life (video game)") (1998), the pathfinding algorithm sometimes failed to find a reasonable way for all the NPCs to evade a thrown grenade; rather than allow the NPCs to attempt to bumble out of the way and risk appearing stupid, the developers instead scripted the NPCs to crouch down and cover in place in that situation.[[29]](#cite_note-29)

#### Video game combat AI

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Artificial_intelligence_in_video_games&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Video game combat AI")]

Many contemporary video games fall under the category of action, [first-person shooter](/wiki/First-person_shooter "First-person shooter"), or adventure. In most of these types of games, there is some level of combat that takes place. The AI's ability to be efficient in combat is important in these genres. A common goal today is to make the AI more human or at least appear so.

One of the more positive and efficient features found in modern-day video game AI is the ability to hunt. AI originally reacted in a very black and white manner. If the player were in a specific area then the AI would react in either a complete offensive manner or be entirely defensive. In recent years, the idea of "hunting" has been introduced; in this 'hunting' state the AI will look for realistic markers, such as sounds made by the character or footprints they may have left behind.[[30]](#cite_note-depot-30) These developments ultimately allow for a more complex form of play. With this feature, the player can actually consider how to approach or avoid an enemy. This is a feature that is particularly prevalent in the [stealth](/wiki/Stealth_game "Stealth game") genre.

Another development in recent game AI has been the development of "survival instinct". In-game computers can recognize different objects in an environment and determine whether it is beneficial or detrimental to its survival. Like a user, the AI can look for cover in a firefight before taking actions that would leave it otherwise vulnerable, such as reloading a weapon or throwing a grenade. There can be set markers that tell it when to react in a certain way. For example, if the AI is given a command to check its health throughout a game then further commands can be set so that it reacts a specific way at a certain percentage of health. If the health is below a certain threshold then the AI can be set to run away from the player and avoid it until another function is triggered. Another example could be if the AI notices it is out of bullets, it will find a cover object and hide behind it until it has reloaded. Actions like these make the AI seem more human. However, there is still a need for improvement in this area.

Another side-effect of combat AI occurs when two AI-controlled characters encounter each other; first popularized in the [id Software](/wiki/Id_Software "Id Software") game *[Doom](/wiki/Doom_(series) "Doom (series)")*, so-called 'monster infighting' can break out in certain situations. Specifically, AI agents that are programmed to respond to hostile attacks will sometimes attack *each other* if their cohort's attacks land too close to them.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] In the case of *Doom*, published gameplay manuals even suggest taking advantage of monster infighting in order to survive certain levels and difficulty settings.

### Procedural content generation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Artificial_intelligence_in_video_games&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Procedural content generation")]

[Procedural content generation](/wiki/Procedural_generation "Procedural generation") (PCG) is an AI technique to autonomously create ingame content through [algorithms](/wiki/Algorithm "Algorithm") with minimal input from designers.[[31]](#cite_note-Todd-2023-31) PCG is typically used to dynamically generate game features such as levels, NPC dialogue, and sounds. Developers input specific parameters to guide the algorithms into making content for them. PCG offers numerous advantages from both a developmental and player experience standpoint. Game studios are able to spend less money on artists and save time on production.[[32]](#cite_note-32) Players are given a fresh, highly replayable experience as the game generates new content each time they play. PCG allows game content to adapt in real time to the player's actions.[[33]](#cite_note-Seidel-33)

#### Procedurally generated levels

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Artificial_intelligence_in_video_games&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Procedurally generated levels")]

Generative algorithms (a rudimentary form of AI) have been used for level creation for decades. The iconic 1980 [dungeon crawler](/wiki/Dungeon_crawl "Dungeon crawl") computer game [*Rogue*](/wiki/Rogue_(video_game) "Rogue (video game)") is a foundational example. Players are tasked with descending through the increasingly difficult levels of a dungeon to retrieve the Amulet of Yendor. The dungeon levels are algorithmically generated at the start of each game. The save file is deleted every time the player dies.[[34]](#cite_note-Liapis-2021-34) The algorithmic dungeon generation creates unique gameplay that would not otherwise be there as the goal of retrieving the amulet is the same each time.

Opinions on total level generation as seen in games like *Rogue* can vary. Some developers can be skeptical of the quality of generated content and desire to create a world with a more "human" feel so they will use PCG more sparingly.[[31]](#cite_note-Todd-2023-31) Consequently, they will only use PCG to generate specific components of an otherwise handcrafted level. A notable example of this is [Ubisoft's](/wiki/Ubisoft "Ubisoft") 2017 [tactical shooter](/wiki/Tactical_shooter "Tactical shooter") *[Tom Clancy's Ghost Recon Wildlands](/wiki/Tom_Clancy%27s_Ghost_Recon_Wildlands "Tom Clancy's Ghost Recon Wildlands")*. Developers used a [pathfinding algorithm](/wiki/Pathfinding "Pathfinding") trained with a [data set](/wiki/Data_set "Data set") of real maps to create road networks that would weave through handcrafted villages within the game world.[[33]](#cite_note-Seidel-33) This is an intelligent use of PCG as the AI would have a large amount of real world data to work with and roads are straightforward to create. However, the AI would likely miss nuances and subtleties if it was tasked with creating a village where people live.

As AI has become more advanced, developer goals are shifting to create massive repositories of levels from data sets. In 2023, researchers from [New York University](/wiki/New_York_University "New York University") and the [University of the Witwatersrand](/wiki/University_of_the_Witwatersrand "University of the Witwatersrand") trained a [large language model](/wiki/Large_language_model "Large language model") to generate levels in the style of the 1981 [puzzle game](/wiki/Puzzle_video_game "Puzzle video game") *[Sokoban](/wiki/Sokoban "Sokoban")*. They found that the model excelled at generating levels with specifically requested characteristics such as difficulty level or layout.[[31]](#cite_note-Todd-2023-31) However, current models such as the one used in the study require large datasets of levels to be effective. They concluded that, while promising, the high data cost of large language models currently outweighs the benefits for this application.[[31]](#cite_note-Todd-2023-31) Continued advancements in the field will likely lead to more mainstream use in the future.

#### Procedurally generated music and sound

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Artificial_intelligence_in_video_games&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Procedurally generated music and sound")]

The [musical score](/wiki/Video_game_music "Video game music") of a video game is an important expression of the emotional tone of a scene to the player. [Sound effects](/wiki/Sound_effect "Sound effect") such as the noise of a weapon hitting an enemy help indicate the effect of the player's actions. Generating these in real time creates an engaging experience for the player because the game is more responsive to their input.[[31]](#cite_note-Todd-2023-31) An example is the 2013 [adventure game](/wiki/Adventure_game "Adventure game") [*Proteus*](/wiki/Proteus_(video_game) "Proteus (video game)") where an algorithm dynamically adapts the music based on the angle the player is viewing the ingame landscape from.[[34]](#cite_note-Liapis-2021-34)

Recent breakthroughs in AI have resulted in the creation of advanced tools that are capable of creating music and sound based on evolving factors with minimal developer input. One such example is the MetaComposure music generator. MetaComposure is an [evolutionary algorithm](/wiki/Evolutionary_algorithm "Evolutionary algorithm") designed to generate original music compositions during real time gameplay to match the current mood of the environment.[[35]](#cite_note-35) The algorithm is able to assess the current mood of the game state through "mood tagging". Research indicates that there is a significant [positive statistical correlation](/wiki/Positive_correlation "Positive correlation") regarding player rated game engagement and the dynamically generated musical compositions when they accurately match their current emotions.[[36]](#cite_note-36)

##### Large language and diffusion models

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Artificial_intelligence_in_video_games&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Large language and diffusion models")]

Generative tools similar to those used in LLMs and diffusion models like Midjourney and DALL-E have been used to generate in-game environments, and companies like Ubisoft have stated that they are experimenting with using AI to generate basic dialogue.[[37]](#cite_note-37) Developers at Roblox are using AI to generate 3D objects for players dubbed Cube 3D, an open-source AI that generates 3D objects based on the methods of LLMs to generate words.[[38]](#cite_note-38)

### Monte Carlo tree search method

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Artificial_intelligence_in_video_games&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Monte Carlo tree search method")]

Game AI often amounts to pathfinding and finite-state machines. Pathfinding gets the AI from point A to point B, usually in the most direct way possible. State machines permit transitioning between different behaviors. The [Monte Carlo tree search](/wiki/Monte_Carlo_tree_search "Monte Carlo tree search") method[[39]](#cite_note-39) provides a more engaging game experience by creating additional obstacles for the player to overcome. The MCTS consists of a tree diagram in which the AI essentially plays [tic-tac-toe](/wiki/Tic-tac-toe "Tic-tac-toe"). Depending on the outcome, it selects a pathway yielding the next obstacle for the player. In complex video games, these trees may have more branches, provided that the player can come up with several strategies to surpass the obstacle.

### Uses in games beyond NPCs

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Artificial_intelligence_in_video_games&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Uses in games beyond NPCs")]

Academic AI may play a role within game AI, outside the traditional concern of controlling NPC behavior. [Georgios N. Yannakakis](/wiki/Georgios_N._Yannakakis "Georgios N. Yannakakis") highlighted four potential application areas:[[17]](#cite_note-Yannakakis2012-17)

1. [Player-experience modeling](/wiki/Player-experience_modeling "Player-experience modeling"): Discerning the ability and emotional state of the player, so as to tailor the game appropriately. This can include [dynamic game difficulty balancing](/wiki/Dynamic_game_difficulty_balancing "Dynamic game difficulty balancing"), which consists in adjusting the difficulty in a video game in real-time based on the player's ability. Game AI may also help deduce player intent (such as [gesture recognition](/wiki/Gesture_recognition "Gesture recognition")).
2. [Procedural-content generation](/wiki/Procedural_generation "Procedural generation"): Creating elements of the game environment like environmental conditions, levels, and even music in an automated way. AI methods can generate new content or interactive stories.
3. [Data mining](/wiki/Data_mining "Data mining") on user behavior: This allows game designers to explore how people use the game, what parts they play most, and what causes them to stop playing, allowing developers to tune gameplay or improve monetization.
4. Alternate approaches to NPCs: These include changing the game set-up to enhance NPC believability and exploring social rather than individual NPC behavior.

Rather than procedural generation, some researchers have used [generative adversarial networks](/wiki/Generative_adversarial_network "Generative adversarial network") (GANs) to create new content. In 2018 researchers at Cornwall University trained a GAN on a thousand human-created levels for *[Doom](/wiki/Doom_(1993_video_game) "Doom (1993 video game)")*; following training, the neural net prototype was able to design new playable levels on its own. Similarly, researchers at the [University of California](/wiki/University_of_California "University of California") prototyped a GAN to generate levels for [Super Mario](/wiki/Super_Mario "Super Mario").[[40]](#cite_note-40) In 2020 Nvidia displayed a GAN-created clone of *[Pac-Man](/wiki/Pac-Man "Pac-Man")*; the GAN learned how to recreate the game by watching 50,000 (mostly bot-generated) playthroughs.[[41]](#cite_note-41)

## Non-player characters (NPCs)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Artificial_intelligence_in_video_games&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Non-player characters (NPCs)")]

[Non-player characters](/wiki/Non-player_character "Non-player character") are entities within video games that are not controlled by players, but instead are managed by AI systems. NPCs contribute to the immersion, storytelling, and the mechanics of a game. They often serve as companions, quest-givers, merchants and much more. Their realism has advanced significantly in the past few years, thanks to improvements in AI technologies.

### Narratives and gameplay roles

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Artificial_intelligence_in_video_games&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Narratives and gameplay roles")]

NPCs are essential in both narrative-driven as well as open-world games. They help convey the lore and context of the game, making them pivotal to world-building and narrative progression. For instance, an NPC can provide critical information, offer quests, or simply populate the world to add a sense of realism to the game. Additionally, their role as quest-givers or merchants makes them integral to the gameplay loop, giving players access to resources, missions, or services that enable further progression.

Additionally, NPCs can be designed to serve functional roles in games, such as a merchant or to provide a service to the player. These characters are central to facilitating game mechanics by acting as intermediaries between the player and in-game systems. Academics [*[who?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Words_to_watch#Unsupported_attributions "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Words to watch")*] say the interactions between players and NPCs are often designed to be straightforward but contextually relevant, ensuring that the player receives necessary feedback or resources for gameplay continuity.

### Advancements in NPC artificial intelligence

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Artificial_intelligence_in_video_games&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Advancements in NPC artificial intelligence")]

Recent advancements[*[as of?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Dates_and_numbers#Chronological_items "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Dates and numbers")*] in artificial intelligence have significantly enhanced the complexity and realism of NPCs. Before these advancements, AI operated on pre-programmed behaviors, making them predictable and repeatable. With AI developing NPCs have become more adaptive and able to dynamically respond to players. Experts[*[who?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Words_to_watch#Unsupported_attributions "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Words to watch")*] think the integration of deep learning and reinforcement learning techniques has enabled NPCs to adjust their behavior in response to player actions, creating a more interactive and personalized gameplay experience.

One such development is the use of adaptive behavior models. These allow NPCs to analyze and learn from players decisions in real time. This behavior allows for a much more engaging experience. For example as said by experts in the field, [*[who?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Words_to_watch#Unsupported_attributions "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Words to watch")*] NPCs in modern video games can now react to player actions with increased sophistication, such as adjusting their tactics in combat or changing their dialogue based on past interactions. By using deep learning algorithms these systems emulate human-like decisions-making, thus making NPCs feel more like real people rather than static game elements.

Another advancements in NPC AI is the use of [natural language processing](/wiki/Natural_language_processing "Natural language processing"), which allows NPCs to engage in more realistic conversations with players. Before this NPC dialogue was limited to a fixed set of responses. It is said [*[by whom?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Words_to_watch#Unsupported_attributions "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Words to watch")*] that NLP has improved the fluidity of NPC conversations, allowing them to respond more contextually to player inputs. This development has increased the depth and immersion of player-NPC interactions, as players can now engage in more complex dialogues that affect the storyline and gameplay outcomes.

Additionally, deep learning models have allowed NPCs to become more capable of predicting players behaviors. Deep learning allows NPCs to process large amounts of data and adapt to player strategies, making interactions with them less predictable and more varied. This creates a more immersive experience, as NPCs are now able to "learn" from player behavior, which provides a greater sense of realism within the game.

### Challenges in NPC development

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Artificial_intelligence_in_video_games&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Challenges in NPC development")]

Despite all of these advancements in NPC AI, there are still significant challenges that developers face in designing NPCs. They need to balance realism, functionally, and players expectations. The key challenge is to make sure that NPCs enhance the players experience, rather than disturb the gameplay. Overly realistic NPCs that behave unpredictably can frustrate players by hindering progression or breaking immersion. Conversely, NPCs that are too predictable or simplistic may fail to engage players, reducing the overall effectiveness of the game's narrative and mechanics.

Another factor that needs to be accounted for is the computation cost of implementing advanced AI for NPCs. The use of these Advanced AI techniques requires large amount of processing power, which can limit its usage. Balancing the performance of AI-driven NPCs with the game's overall technical limitations is crucial for ensuring smooth gameplay. Experts [*[who?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Words_to_watch#Unsupported_attributions "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Words to watch")*] mentioned how developers must allocate resources efficiently to avoid overburdening the game’s systems, particularly in large, open-world games where numerous NPCs must interact with the player simultaneously.

Finally, creating NPCs that can respond dynamically to a wide range of player behaviors remains a difficult task. NPCs must be able to handle both scripted interactions and unscripted scenarios where players may behave in unexpected ways. Designing NPCs capable of adapting to such variability requires complex AI models that can account for numerous possible interactions, which can be resource-intensive and time-consuming for developers.

## Cheating AI

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Artificial_intelligence_in_video_games&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Cheating AI")]

> Gamers always ask if the AI cheats (presumably so they can complain if they lose)

— Terry Lee Coleman of *Computer Gaming World*, 1994[[42]](#cite_note-coleman199407-42)

In the context of artificial intelligence in video games, cheating refers to the programmer giving agents actions and access to information that would be unavailable to the player in the same situation.[[43]](#cite_note-Scott_2002-43) Believing that the [Atari 8-bit](/wiki/Atari_8-bit "Atari 8-bit") could not compete against a human player, [Chris Crawford](/wiki/Chris_Crawford_(game_designer) "Chris Crawford (game designer)") did not fix a bug in *[Eastern Front (1941)](/wiki/Eastern_Front_(1941) "Eastern Front (1941)")* that benefited the computer-controlled Russian side.[[44]](#cite_note-cgw199407-44) *Computer Gaming World* in 1994 reported that "It is a well-known fact that many AIs 'cheat' (or, at least, 'fudge') in order to be able to keep up with human players".[[45]](#cite_note-wilson199402-45)

For example, if the agents want to know if the player is nearby they can either be given complex, human-like sensors (seeing, hearing, etc.), or they can cheat by simply asking the [game engine](/wiki/Game_engine "Game engine") for the player's position. Common variations include giving AIs higher speeds in racing games to catch up to the player or spawning them in advantageous positions in first-person shooters. The use of cheating in AI shows the limitations of the "intelligence" achievable artificially; generally speaking, in games where strategic creativity is important, humans could easily beat the AI after a minimum of trial and error if it were not for this advantage. Cheating is often implemented for performance reasons where in many cases it may be considered acceptable as long as the effect is not obvious to the player. While cheating refers only to privileges given specifically to the AI—it does not include the inhuman swiftness and precision natural to a computer—a player might call the computer's inherent advantages "cheating" if they result in the agent acting unlike a human player.[[43]](#cite_note-Scott_2002-43) [Sid Meier](/wiki/Sid_Meier "Sid Meier") stated that he omitted multiplayer alliances in *[Civilization](/wiki/Civilization_(video_game) "Civilization (video game)")* because he found that the computer was almost as good as humans in using them, which caused players to think that the computer was cheating.[[46]](#cite_note-cgw199307-46) Developers say that most game AIs are honest but they dislike players erroneously complaining about "cheating" AI. In addition, humans use tactics against computers that they would not against other people.[[44]](#cite_note-cgw199407-44)

In short, cheating AI is when the game seems to break its own rules, producing impossible outcomes. The opposite is [dynamic difficulty](/wiki/Dynamic_game_difficulty_balancing#Uses_in_video_games "Dynamic game difficulty balancing"), a structured adjustment of AI skill to maintain competition, respecting game mechanics. Some games may have a blend of both of these systems at once.

## Examples

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Artificial_intelligence_in_video_games&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Examples")]

In the 1996 game *[Creatures](/wiki/Creatures_(artificial_life_program) "Creatures (artificial life program)")*, the user "hatches" small furry animals and teaches them how to behave. These "Norns" can talk, feed themselves, and protect themselves against vicious creatures. It was the first popular application of machine learning in an interactive simulation. [Neural networks](/wiki/Neural_network_(machine_learning) "Neural network (machine learning)") are used by the creatures to learn what to do. The game is regarded as a breakthrough in [artificial life](/wiki/Artificial_life "Artificial life") research, which aims to model the behavior of creatures interacting with their environment.[[47]](#cite_note-AiGameDev-47)

In the 2001 [first-person shooter](/wiki/First-person_shooter "First-person shooter") *[Halo: Combat Evolved](/wiki/Halo:_Combat_Evolved "Halo: Combat Evolved")* the player assumes the role of the Master Chief, battling various aliens on foot or in vehicles. Enemies use cover very wisely, and employ suppressing fire and grenades. The squad situation affects the individuals, so certain enemies flee when their leader dies. Attention is paid to the little details, with enemies notably throwing back grenades or team-members responding to being bothered. The underlying "[behavior tree](/wiki/Behavior_tree_(artificial_intelligence,_robotics_and_control) "Behavior tree (artificial intelligence, robotics and control)")" technology has become very popular in the games industry since *[Halo 2](/wiki/Halo_2 "Halo 2")*.[[47]](#cite_note-AiGameDev-47)

The 2005 [psychological horror](/wiki/Psychological_horror "Psychological horror") first-person shooter *[F.E.A.R.](/wiki/F.E.A.R._(video_game) "F.E.A.R. (video game)")* has player characters engage a [battalion](/wiki/Battalion "Battalion") of cloned [super-soldiers](/wiki/Supersoldier "Supersoldier"), robots and [paranormal creatures](/wiki/Paranormal "Paranormal"). The AI uses a planner to generate context-sensitive behaviors, the first time in a mainstream game. This technology is still used as a reference for many studios. The Replicas are capable of utilizing the game environment to their advantage, such as overturning tables and shelves to create cover, opening doors, crashing through windows, or even noticing (and alerting the rest of their comrades to) the player's flashlight. In addition, the AI is also capable of performing flanking maneuvers, using suppressing fire, throwing grenades to flush the player out of cover, and even playing dead. Most of these actions, in particular the flanking, is the result of emergent behavior.[[48]](#cite_note-48)[[49]](#cite_note-49)

The [survival horror](/wiki/Survival_horror "Survival horror") series *[S.T.A.L.K.E.R.](/wiki/S.T.A.L.K.E.R. "S.T.A.L.K.E.R.")* (2007–) confronts the player with man-made experiments, military soldiers, and mercenaries known as Stalkers. The various encountered enemies (if the difficulty level is set to its highest) use combat tactics and behaviors such as healing wounded allies, giving orders, out-flanking the player and using weapons with pinpoint accuracy.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The 2010 [real-time strategy](/wiki/Real-time_strategy "Real-time strategy") game *[StarCraft II: Wings of Liberty](/wiki/StarCraft_II:_Wings_of_Liberty "StarCraft II: Wings of Liberty")* gives the player control of one of three factions in a 1v1, 2v2, or 3v3 battle arena. The player must defeat their opponents by destroying all their units and bases. This is accomplished by creating units that are effective at countering opponents' units. Players can play against multiple different levels of AI difficulty ranging from very easy to Cheater 3 (insane). The AI is able to cheat at the difficulty Cheater 1 (vision), where it can see units and bases when a player in the same situation could not. Cheater 2 gives the AI extra resources, while Cheater 3 gives an extensive advantage over its opponent.[[50]](#cite_note-50)

*Red Dead Redemption 2*, released by Rockstar Games in 2018, exemplifies the advanced use of AI in modern video games. The game incorporates a highly detailed AI system that governs the behavior of NPCs and the dynamic game world. NPCs in the game display complex and varied behaviors based on a wide range of factors including their environment, player interactions, and time of day. This level of AI integration creates a rich, immersive experience where characters react to players in a realistic manner, contributing to the game's reputation as one of the most advanced open-world games ever created.[[51]](#cite_note-51)

## Generative artificial intelligence in video games

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Artificial_intelligence_in_video_games&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Generative artificial intelligence in video games")]

[Generative artificial intelligence](/wiki/Generative_artificial_intelligence "Generative artificial intelligence"), AI systems that can respond to prompts and produce text, images, and audio and video clips, arose in 2023 with systems like [ChatGPT](/wiki/ChatGPT "ChatGPT") and [Stable Diffusion](/wiki/Stable_Diffusion "Stable Diffusion"). In video games, these systems could create the potential for game assets to be created indefinitely, bypassing typical limitations on human creations.

For example, the 2024 [browser-based](/wiki/Browser_game "Browser game") [sandbox](/wiki/Sandbox_game "Sandbox game") game *[Infinite Craft](/wiki/Infinite_Craft "Infinite Craft")* uses [generative AI](/wiki/Generative_artificial_intelligence "Generative artificial intelligence") software, including [LLaMA](/wiki/LLaMA "LLaMA"). When two elements are being combined, a new element is generated by the AI.[[52]](#cite_note-52)
The 2024 browser-based game *[Oasis](/wiki/Oasis_(Minecraft_clone) "Oasis (Minecraft clone)")* uses generative AI to simulate the video game *[Minecraft](/wiki/Minecraft "Minecraft")*. *Oasis* is trained on millions of hours of footage from *Minecraft*, and predicts how the next frame of gameplay looks using this dataset. *Oasis* does not have object permanence because it does not store any data.[[53]](#cite_note-53) The 2025 video game *[AI2U: With You 'Til The End](/wiki/AI2U:_With_You_%27Til_The_End "AI2U: With You 'Til The End")*, developed by AlterStaff Inc., used ChatGPT for the non-playable characters dialogue in the game.[[54]](#cite_note-IGWSC-54)

However, there are similar [concerns](/wiki/Generative_artificial_intelligence#Concerns "Generative artificial intelligence") in other fields particularly the potential for loss of jobs normally dedicated to the creation of these assets.[[55]](#cite_note-55) To assure such content was disclosed by game publishers and avoid potential copyright issues, [Valve](/wiki/Valve_Corporation "Valve Corporation") implemented new requirements for games on [Steam](/wiki/Steam_(service) "Steam (service)") that the use of pre-generated AI assets or live generation via generative AI must be disclosed on the storefront page.[[56]](#cite_note-56)

Voice actors were one of the first groups to express concern on generative AI use in video games. The North American actor's union [SAG-AFTRA](/wiki/SAG-AFTRA "SAG-AFTRA") had finished a [strike in 2023](/wiki/2023_SAG-AFTRA_strike "2023 SAG-AFTRA strike") against television and film production companies, with the strike concluded on new contract terms that gave actors strong control over whether their voices could be used to train generative AI engines.[[57]](#cite_note-57) Shortly afterward, the union agreed to a contract with AI company Replica Studios to allow union voices to be used to train AI and used in games, with the contract assuring pay and rights protections. While the contract was agreed upon by a SAG-AFTRA committee, many members expressed criticism of the move, having not been told of it until it was completed and that the deal did not do enough to protect the actors.[[58]](#cite_note-58) By the time that multiple video game publishers were set to renew terms of their general contact with SAG-AFTRA, the union had taken a stronger stance, desiring more protections for actors against AI use, and began a nearly year-long strike in July 2024 when the video game companies did not concede to these issues. The strike concluded by July 2025 with the affected publishers agreeing to a contract that gave actors stronger control of whether their voices could be used for AI.[[59]](#cite_note-59)[[60]](#cite_note-60)[[61]](#cite_note-61)

*[ARC Raiders](/wiki/ARC_Raiders "ARC Raiders")*, released by [Embark Studios](/wiki/Embark_Studios "Embark Studios") in November 2025, was found to use generative AI voices for some of the NPCs in the game. While the game was generally well-received by most gaming outlets with an 86 out of 100 aggregate score on [Metacritic](/wiki/Metacritic "Metacritic"), *[Eurogamer](/wiki/Eurogamer "Eurogamer")* gave it one of the worst scores, rating it 2 out of 5 and specifically penalizing the game due to the use of generative AI voices. This began more discussion on the matter of AI voices, with players critical of the studio turning to AI voices, while others, like [Tim Sweeney](/wiki/Tim_Sweeney "Tim Sweeney"), said that there is a great deal of potential for using AI voices to have a near infinite ways for NPC in games to speak to players.[[62]](#cite_note-62)

Artificial intelligence has been included to perform [video upscaling](/wiki/Video_scaler "Video scaler") of game images. Both NVidia's [Deep Learning Super Sampling](/wiki/Deep_Learning_Super_Sampling "Deep Learning Super Sampling") (DLSS) and AMD's [FidelityFX Super Resolution](/wiki/FidelityFX_Super_Resolution "FidelityFX Super Resolution") (FSR) have included AI as part of their engine. In March 2026, NVidia announced version 5.0 of DLSS to release later in 2026, which among other improvements, includes an AI engine that "infuses pixels with photoreal lighting and materials" as to make existing game graphics appear more realistic. While some developers, including [Todd Howard](/wiki/Todd_Howard "Todd Howard") of Bethesda Softworks and [Jun Takeuchi](/wiki/Jun_Takeuchi "Jun Takeuchi") of Capcom praised the technology, it was met by other developers and many players as appearing like [AI slop](/wiki/AI_slop "AI slop"), with character faces seemingly normalized to the appearance of other human characters created by generative AI video output or video filters used on social media, and taking away from the developers' artistic efforts.[[63]](#cite_note-63) NVidia's [Jensen Huang](/wiki/Jensen_Huang "Jensen Huang") said that developers will far more control over how DLSS 5.0 would interact with their games, allowing the developer to tweak lighting and texture adjustments, and that NVidia was continuing to improve the technology before its release.[[64]](#cite_note-64)

## Advancements in AI

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Artificial_intelligence_in_video_games&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Advancements in AI")]

Recent advancements in AI for video games have led to more complex and adaptive behaviors in non-playable characters (NPCs). For instance, AI systems now utilize sophisticated techniques such as decision trees and state machines to enhance NPC interactions and realism, as discussed in "Artificial Intelligence in Games".[[65]](#cite_note-65) Recent advancements in AI for video games have also focused on improving dynamic and adaptive behaviors in NPCs. For example, recent research has explored the use of complex neural networks to enable NPCs to learn and adapt their behavior based on player actions, enhancing the overall gaming experience. This approach is detailed in the IEEE paper on "AI Techniques for Interactive Game Systems".[[66]](#cite_note-66)
